location.origin: Memphis, Tennessee (born November 12, 1944, as Booker Taliaferro Jones Jr.). Middle-class Black family; grew up on Ethel Avenue in South Memphis, neighborhood now known as Soulsville. Father was a science teacher. Musical prodigy: learned saxophone, trombone, oboe, double bass, piano at school; played organ at church. Attended Booker T. Washington High School. Classmate and best friend of Maurice White (future Earth, Wind & Fire founder); formed high school jazz combo with White and David Porter (future Isaac Hayes songwriting partner).
location.formative: Stax Records, 926 East McLemore Avenue, Memphis (1960-1971). Recruited as staff musician at age 16 to play baritone saxophone on 'Cause I Love You' by Carla and Rufus Thomas (1960). Formed Booker T. & the M.G.'s (1962) with Steve Cropper (guitar), Lewie Steinberg (bass, replaced by Donald 'Duck' Dunn in 1965), and Al Jackson Jr. (drums). House band for Stax -- played on hundreds of sessions for Otis Redding, Sam & Dave, Albert King, Wilson Pickett, Carla Thomas, Eddie Floyd, and others. Earned Bachelor of Music Education from Indiana University while commuting to Memphis for sessions on weekends.
location.career: M.G.'s era (1962-1971): 'Green Onions' (1962, No. 1 R&B, No. 3 Pop), 'Hip Hug-Her' (1967), 'Soul Limbo' (1968), 'Time Is Tight' (1969), 'Melting Pot' (1971). Solo/production era (1971-present): Produced Bill Withers's Just as I Am (1971), Rita Coolidge's Love Me Again (1978), Willie Nelson's Stardust (1978 -- Nelson's biggest-selling album, 10 years on Billboard country chart). Solo albums include Potato Hole (2009, Grammy Best Instrumental Album with Drive-By Truckers and Neil Young), Sound the Alarm (2013, on Stax Records). Memoir Time Is Tight: My Life, Note by Note (2019). Currently touring worldwide with solo band, including '60 Years of Green Onions' concert series (2022-present). Seattle-area performances: Triple Door (April 2022, October 2025), Kirkland Performance Center (April 2024), Mount Baker Theatre Bellingham (October 2025).

formed: 1962 (Booker T. & the M.G.'s, Memphis; solo career from 1971 onward)
labels: Stax Records (1962-1975; Green Onions through Melting Pot; Sound the Alarm, 2013)

members.name: Booker T. Jones
members.role: Hammond B-3 organ, piano, multi-instrumentalist, producer
members.note: Also proficient on saxophone, trombone, oboe, double bass, guitar. Bachelor of Music Education from Indiana University (senior recital on trombone). Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award (2007). Grammy Best Instrumental Album (2010). Rock and Roll Hall of Fame (1992). Memphis Music Hall of Fame. Arguably the most famous Hammond B3 player in history.

members.name: Steve Cropper
members.note: M.G.'s co-founder. Co-wrote '(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay' with Otis Redding, 'In the Midnight Hour' with Wilson Pickett. From the Missouri Ozarks -- white member of the integrated M.G.'s. Rock and Roll Hall of Fame (1992). Songwriters Hall of Fame (2005). Died December 3, 2025, Nashville, age 84.

members.name: Donald 'Duck' Dunn
members.note: Replaced Lewie Steinberg in 1965. Syncopated bass style replaced Steinberg's walking lines. Played on hundreds of Stax sessions. Also known for The Blues Brothers band. Died May 13, 2012, Tokyo, age 70.

members.name: Al Jackson Jr.
members.note: Original M.G.'s member. 'The Human Timekeeper.' Precision, restraint, groove. Shot and killed October 1, 1975, Memphis, age 39. Circumstances disputed.

members.name: Lewie Steinberg
members.note: Original bassist on 'Green Onions' and early M.G.'s recordings. Walking bass style. Replaced by Duck Dunn in 1965. Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award (2007) alongside other M.G.'s members.

associated_musicians.name: Otis Redding
associated_musicians.note: The M.G.'s provided the foundation for Redding's greatest recordings: 'Try a Little Tenderness,' 'Respect,' 'I've Been Loving You Too Long,' '(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay.' Cropper co-wrote 'Dock of the Bay' with Redding. Redding died in a plane crash December 10, 1967.

associated_musicians.name: Sam & Dave
associated_musicians.note: 'Hold On, I'm Comin',' 'Soul Man,' 'I Thank You.' Sam Moore and Dave Prater. The M.G.'s groove on 'Soul Man' is one of the most recognizable rhythm tracks in American music.

associated_musicians.name: Albert King
associated_musicians.note: 'Born Under a Bad Sign,' 'Crosscut Saw.' Blues guitarist whose Stax recordings with the M.G.'s bridged blues and soul.

associated_musicians.name: Wilson Pickett
associated_musicians.note: 'In the Midnight Hour,' '634-5789,' 'Don't Fight It.' Recorded at Stax with M.G.'s backing. Cropper co-wrote 'In the Midnight Hour.'

associated_musicians.name: Willie Nelson
associated_musicians.note: Jones produced Stardust (1978) -- Nelson's biggest-selling album. They were neighbors in Malibu. Ten days of recording. Ten years on Billboard country chart. A Memphis soul organist producing a country outlaw's pop standards album.

associated_musicians.name: Bill Withers
associated_musicians.note: Jones produced Just as I Am (1971), Withers's debut album containing 'Ain't No Sunshine.' Jones played guitar as well as keyboards on the album.

associated_musicians.name: Maurice White
associated_musicians.note: Best friend at Booker T. Washington High School. Founded Earth, Wind & Fire. They formed a jazz combo in high school with David Porter.

associated_musicians.name: David Porter
associated_musicians.note: High school jazz combo with Jones and White. Became Isaac Hayes's songwriting partner at Stax -- the Hayes/Porter team wrote 'Soul Man' and 'Hold On, I'm Comin'.'

associated_musicians.name: Neil Young
associated_musicians.note: Featured on Jones's Grammy-winning album Potato Hole (2009). Cross-genre collaboration linking Memphis soul to Canadian rock.

associated_musicians.name: Ray Charles
associated_musicians.note: Degree 30. Recorded for Atlantic Records, which distributed Stax nationally (1960-1968). The Atlantic-Stax deal connects their label homes within the engine.

associated_musicians.name: Quincy Jones
associated_musicians.note: Degree 0. Both are multi-instrumentalists and producers who reshaped American music from behind the scenes. Quincy left Seattle; Booker T. visits Seattle. Memphis-to-Seattle pipeline, reversed.

theory_nodes.concept: The Touring Anchor (thirty-ninth artist-city pattern -- the artist from another city whose repeated performances in Seattle create an ongoing gravitational relationship between two musical traditions; the Touring Anchor does not relocate or claim Seattle as home; the Touring Anchor returns, repeatedly, because there is an audience that understands what the music means and because the artist's tradition and the city's tradition are branches of the same tree; the first non-residential artist-city pattern in the engine)
examples: Booker T. Jones (degree 38) -- Memphis-born, performs repeatedly at The Triple Door (Seattle), Kirkland Performance Center, Mount Baker Theatre (Bellingham). Not a Seattle resident. The parenthetical '(Seattle shows)' in the CSV explicitly frames this as a touring relationship. Memphis soul tradition (Stax, M.G.'s) structurally connected to Seattle's Central District R&B/soul scene through shared roots in the Great Migration, gospel tradition, and blues.
Contrast with The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- the Export leaves the city permanently. The Touring Anchor never lived in the city.
Contrast with The Incubator (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- the Incubator passed through briefly and was shaped by the city. The Touring Anchor visits repeatedly but was shaped elsewhere.
Contrast with The Inheritor (degree 37, Ayron Jones) -- the Inheritor was born and raised in the city. The Touring Anchor was born and raised elsewhere.
The Touring Anchor introduces the concept of gravitational connection: a city's musical significance is defined not only by who lives there but by who returns to perform there.
theory_nodes.description: The Touring Anchor is the thirty-ninth artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes an artist from another city whose repeated performances in Seattle create an ongoing gravitational relationship between two musical traditions. The key distinction from all prior artist-city patterns is the non-residential nature of the connection: the Touring Anchor was not born in Seattle, did not grow up in Seattle, did not form a band in Seattle. The relationship is defined by performance, cultural influence, and structural connection between traditions. Jones's Memphis soul tradition and Seattle's Central District R&B/soul tradition share common roots (Great Migration, gospel, blues), and his repeated performances in Seattle venues affirm that connection across geographic distance.

ancestors: The Incubator (degree 30, Ray Charles) -- passed through and was shaped by the city. The Touring Anchor visits but was shaped elsewhere.
The Export (degree 0, Quincy Jones) -- left the city for a national career. The Touring Anchor never lived in the city.
The Inheritor (degree 37, Ayron Jones) -- born and raised in the city. The Touring Anchor was born elsewhere.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-nine artist-city patterns across thirty-nine degrees. The PNW-to-global taxonomy now has six modes: Export (0), Incubator (30), Deep Root (35), Supernova (36), Inheritor (37), Touring Anchor (38). The Touring Anchor is the first mode defined by absence of residence -- all prior modes assumed the artist lived in Seattle at some point.

theory_nodes.concept: The congregational organ (thirty-ninth acoustic element -- the sound of a Hammond B-3 organ with rotating Leslie speaker cabinet, gospel-rooted, warm and swirling; the harmonic glue of Stax Records; defined by economy and groove rather than speed or volume; the organ fills the harmonic middle of the frequency spectrum between bass and guitar; the first keyboard-based acoustic element in the engine, breaking the three-degree Stratocaster thread)
examples: Booker T. Jones (degree 38) -- Hammond B-3 through Leslie speaker. The opening riff of 'Green Onions' is the canonical example: churning, repetitive, hypnotic organ figure that defines the song without requiring any other harmonic support. Economy is the defining quality -- Jones plays exactly what the song needs and nothing more.
Contrast with stinging clean (degree 35, Robert Cray) -- guitar, clean and precise. The congregational organ is warm and swirling.
Contrast with controlled scream (degree 36, Jimi Hendrix) -- guitar, overdriven and intense. The congregational organ is restrained and groove-locked.
Contrast with dynamic grit (degree 37, Ayron Jones) -- guitar, dynamic contrast. The congregational organ maintains consistent dynamic level within the groove.
The Stratocaster thread (35-36-37) is broken at degree 38. A new timbral family -- keyboard -- enters the engine.
theory_nodes.description: The congregational organ is the thirty-ninth acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the specific timbral quality of a Hammond B-3 organ played through a rotating Leslie speaker cabinet, with roots in the Black church tradition. The Leslie speaker produces a warm, swirling tone through physical rotation -- the sound literally moves through space. Jones's approach is defined by economy and groove: he plays with precision, placing chords and melodic lines in the rhythmic spaces created by the bass and drums. The organ fills the harmonic middle of the frequency spectrum, serving as the glue between bass and guitar. This is the first keyboard-based acoustic element in the engine, breaking the three-consecutive-degree Stratocaster thread (35-36-37).

ancestors: The dynamic grit (degree 37, Ayron Jones) -- guitar through overdriven amplification with dynamics. The congregational organ shifts from guitar to keyboard.
The controlled scream (degree 36, Jimi Hendrix) -- maximum overdriven guitar force. The congregational organ is the opposite timbral approach: warm, restrained, groove-locked.
The stinging clean (degree 35, Robert Cray) -- clean guitar precision. Both share economy as a defining quality, but the instrument family differs entirely.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-nine acoustic elements across thirty-nine degrees. The Stratocaster thread (35-36-37) ends at degree 38. A new instrumental family (keyboard) enters the engine. The timbral palette shifts from guitar-dominated (degrees 35-37) to organ-driven (degree 38).

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the gospel swell (thirty-eighth deployment; the blue note as it exists on the Hammond B-3 organ -- approached through chord voicing and Leslie speaker rotation rather than string bending or vocal inflection; minor-to-major ambiguity in chord choices placing the flatted third and flatted seventh where they color harmony without resolving; the Leslie speaker's Doppler effect creates natural vibrato making every note slightly blue; the warm communal wash hovering between sadness and joy, between secular and sacred)
examples: Booker T. Jones (degree 38) -- the opening riff of 'Green Onions' contains blue notes embedded in the organ's timbre. The instrument does the bending through Leslie speaker rotation (Doppler effect). The player provides the intention through chord voicing.
Contrast with grunge bend (degree 37, Ayron Jones) -- guitar string bend shaped by dynamics. The gospel swell is not bent through string tension but through chord voicing and speaker physics.
Contrast with screaming bend (degree 36, Hendrix) -- maximum saturation guitar bend. The gospel swell is warm and communal, not individual and intense.
Contrast with articulate sting (degree 35, Cray) -- maximum clarity guitar bend. The gospel swell is swirling and diffuse, not clean and precise.

The gospel swell returns the blue note to the keyboard tradition that existed alongside the guitar tradition throughout blues history. It is a parallel lineage, not a successor to the three consecutive guitar deployments.
theory_nodes.description: Thirty-eighth deployment of the blue note thread. The gospel swell is the blue note as it exists on the Hammond B-3 organ, approached through chord voicing and the physical rotation of the Leslie speaker rather than through string bending or vocal inflection. Jones voices minor-to-major ambiguity in his chord choices, placing the flatted third and seventh where they color harmony without resolving it. The Leslie speaker adds a physical dimension: the Doppler effect from the rotating speaker creates a natural vibrato that makes every note slightly blue. This is the blue note of the Black church translated into secular instrumental music -- the warm communal wash that hovers between sadness and joy.

ancestors: Blue note -- all thirty-seven prior deployments (degrees 0-37)
Blue note -- the grunge bend (degree 37, Ayron Jones): dynamic guitar bend. The gospel swell shifts from string to keyboard.
Blue note -- screaming bend (degree 36, Hendrix): maximum guitar saturation. The gospel swell is warm and restrained.
Blue note -- articulate sting (degree 35, Cray): maximum guitar clarity. The gospel swell is diffuse and swirling.
genealogy.relationship: Thirty-eight deployments across thirty-nine degrees. The gospel swell breaks the three-consecutive guitar-specific blue note arc (35-36-37: articulate sting, screaming bend, grunge bend) and returns the blue note to the keyboard tradition. The organ-based blue note is the first non-guitar, non-vocal blue note deployment since the early degrees.

theory_nodes.concept: Institutional tidal (seventh E=5 mode -- the energy of an artist whose individual contribution is inseparable from the institution they helped build; Jones's E=5 is measured partly through the hundreds of sessions he played, the dozens of artists he backed, the label he helped define, and the sound he helped create; the institution amplifies the individual and the individual gives the institution its voice; the first E=5 mode that foregrounds the collective over the individual)
examples: Booker T. Jones (degree 38) -- E=5 institutional tidal. Rock and Roll Hall of Fame (1992). Grammy Lifetime Achievement (2007). Grammy Best Instrumental Album (2010). Hundreds of Stax sessions. Produced Willie Nelson's Stardust. Energy flows through institutional channels: Stax Records, M.G.'s, session work, production credits.
Contrast with successor tidal (degree 37, Ayron Jones, E=5) -- energy defined by relationship to predecessor. Institutional tidal is defined by relationship to institution.
Contrast with breakthrough tidal (degree 35, Cray, E=5) -- individual escape velocity. Institutional tidal is collective.
Contrast with collective tidal (degree 34, Tempos, E=5) -- group energy within a local scene. Institutional tidal extends beyond the local to national/international institutional impact.
Contrast with personal tidal (degree 32, Rabb, E=5) -- individual career energy. Institutional tidal is defined by the institution, not the individual alone.
Distribution after degree 38: 2:13:6:11:6:1. E=5 count rises to six. E=6 remains singular.
theory_nodes.description: Institutional tidal is the seventh E=5 mode in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the energy of an artist whose individual contribution is inseparable from the institution they helped build. Jones's E=5 flows through institutional channels: Stax Records (the label), the M.G.'s (the house band), hundreds of session credits, production work for other artists. The distinction from other E=5 modes is that the institution amplifies the individual and the individual gives the institution its voice -- they are inseparable. This is the first E=5 mode that foregrounds the collective and institutional over the purely individual.

ancestors: Successor tidal (degree 37, Ayron Jones, E=5) -- defined by predecessor relationship. Institutional tidal is defined by institution relationship.
Breakthrough tidal (degree 35, Cray, E=5) -- individual escape velocity. Institutional tidal is collective-institutional.
Collective tidal (degree 34, Tempos, E=5) -- local group energy. Institutional tidal extends beyond local to national/international.
Scene-cumulative tidal (degree 33, English, E=5) -- scene-level energy. Institutional tidal is label-level.
Personal tidal (degree 32, Rabb, E=5) -- individual career. Institutional tidal adds the institution.
genealogy.relationship: Seven E=5 modes plus one E=6 mode across seven degrees (32-38). The progression: personal (32) -> scene (33) -> collective (34) -> breakthrough (35) -> transformative/E=6 (36) -> successor (37) -> institutional (38). Seven consecutive degrees at E=5 or above. Distribution: 2:13:6:11:6:1.

seattle_connection: TOURING ANCHOR -- the first non-residential artist-city pattern in the engine. Jones is from Memphis, Tennessee; his presence in the engine reflects repeated performances at Seattle/PNW venues (Triple Door April 2022 + October 2025, Kirkland Performance Center April 2024, Mount Baker Theatre Bellingham October 2025) and the structural connection between Memphis soul (Stax Records, M.G.'s) and Seattle's Central District R&B/soul tradition. THE TOURING ANCHOR as 39th artist-city pattern -- gravitational relationship between two musical traditions, defined by performance rather than residence. THE CONGREGATIONAL ORGAN as 39th acoustic element -- Hammond B-3, gospel-rooted, Leslie speaker, breaks 3-degree Stratocaster thread. THE GOSPEL SWELL as 38th blue note deployment -- blue note embedded in organ timbre, Doppler wobble, chord voicing. INSTITUTIONAL TIDAL as 7th E=5 mode -- energy measured through institution (Stax, M.G.'s, hundreds of sessions). R&B/SOUL RETURNS at Genre Stage 19 after Blues Rock at 36-37. STAX RECORDS enters label ecosystem, connecting to Atlantic Records (degree 30) through historical distribution deal. SEVEN CONSECUTIVE DEGREES at E=5 or above (32-38). FIRST KEYBOARD-BASED ACOUSTIC ELEMENT. GREEN ONIONS (1962). MEMPHIS GROUP. HOUSE BAND MODEL. SESSION MUSICIAN AS ARCHITECT. LAST M.G. STANDING -- sole surviving member of classic lineup after Steve Cropper death (December 2025). GENDER: 6:33 ratio (~15.4%). Jones is male.

theory_genealogy: The Touring Anchor (thirty-ninth artist-city pattern; non-residential gravitational connection through repeated performance; artist's tradition and city's tradition are branches of same tree)
The congregational organ (thirty-ninth acoustic element; Hammond B-3, Leslie speaker, gospel-rooted groove; breaks Stratocaster thread; first keyboard element)
Blue note -- the gospel swell (thirty-eighth deployment; organ-based blue note through chord voicing and Leslie speaker Doppler; returns blue note to keyboard tradition)
Institutional tidal (seventh E=5 mode; energy inseparable from institution built; Stax, M.G.'s, hundreds of sessions, production credits)
engine_sequencing: E=5 CONTINUES at 38: Distribution 2:13:6:11:6:1. Institutional tidal as seventh E=5 mode. Seven consecutive degrees at E=5+ (32-38). E=6 remains singular. Track at degree 39.
GENRE Stage 19 at degree 38: R&B/Soul returns after Blues Rock at 36-37. Last R&B/Soul was at 29-30. Genre oscillation continues. Track at degree 39.
CONGREGATIONAL ORGAN breaks Stratocaster thread: 3 consecutive Stratocaster degrees (35-36-37) end. Keyboard enters as new timbral family. Track element 40 at degree 39.
STAX RECORDS x1 (degree 38). New label. Connects to Atlantic Records (degree 30) through historical distribution deal. Track at degree 39.
SEVEN CONSECUTIVE E=5+ degrees (32-38): Longest sustained high-energy sequence in the engine. Track at degree 39 -- will E=5 continue?
FIRST NON-RESIDENTIAL ARTIST: Jones is from Memphis, not Seattle. '(Seattle shows)' framing is new. Track for additional non-residential artists.
SOLO FORMAT continues from 35-36-37, but Jones is fundamentally a band leader/session musician. The 'solo' designation is complicated by the institutional/collaborative nature of his work.
All engine sequencing items from degrees 0-37
cultural_patterns: Artist-city taxonomy: THIRTY-NINE named patterns (adding The Touring Anchor at degree 38). Six PNW-to-global modes: Export, Incubator, Deep Root, Supernova, Inheritor, Touring Anchor. Classify every degree.

MEMPHIS-SEATTLE CONNECTION: Stax Records (Memphis) and Central District scene (Seattle) share roots in Great Migration, gospel, blues. Wheedle's Groove documents Seattle's parallel soul tradition influenced by Stax records.
ATLANTIC-STAX IN ENGINE: Both labels now represented (Atlantic at degree 30, Stax at degree 38). Historical distribution deal (1960-1968) connects them.
SESSION MUSICIAN AS ARCHITECT: Jones's M.G.'s model -- building the sound others inhabit -- is structurally new in the engine. Parallel to Luther Rabb (degree 32) but at institutional scale.
LAST M.G. STANDING: Jones is sole surviving member of classic lineup. Al Jackson Jr. (d. 1975), Duck Dunn (d. 2012), Steve Cropper (d. 2025). Living archive.
INTEGRATION AT STAX: Two Black, two white musicians in 1960s Memphis. Jim Crow defiance through daily musical practice.
E=5 AS SUSTAINED MAXIMUM: Seven consecutive E=5+ degrees (32-38). Six E=5 modes plus one E=6 mode. Track.
All cultural pattern items from degrees 0-37
